title: EMI Is the Disturbance. EMC Is the Evidence. by Dr. Moh Kolbehdari
gist: An engineering explainer distinguishes electromagnetic interference (EMI, the unwanted disturbance) from electromagnetic compatibility (EMC, the system-level evidence a product coexists reliably), arguing EMC must be engineered from the start across IC, package, PCB, and firmware rather than fixed via end-of-line compliance testing.

insights.claim: EMI (electromagnetic interference) is the unwanted electromagnetic energy that can disturb a circuit, product, or system, while EMC (electromagnetic compatibility) is the demonstrated ability of that product to operate reliably in its electromagnetic environment without creating unacceptable interference for others — the two terms are often conflated but are fundamentally different.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A precise conceptual distinction relevant to any hardware/semiconductor design team's compliance strategy; conflating the two leads to under-engineering EMC as a checkbox rather than a system property.
insights.quote: EMI, or electromagnetic interference, is the unwanted electromagnetic energy that can disturb a circuit, product, or system. EMC, or electromagnetic compatibility, is the demonstrated ability of that product or system to operate reliably in its electromagnetic environment
insights.locator: EMI and EMC are often used

insights.claim: A complete interference problem contains three elements — a source, a coupling path, and a victim — and EMI can travel via conducted, radiated, capacitive/inductive, or common-impedance coupling paths.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Provides a structured troubleshooting model (source-path-victim) that hardware engineering teams and their component suppliers can apply to diagnose EMI issues systematically.
insights.quote: A complete interference problem usually contains three elements: A source. A coupling path. A victim.
insights.locator: It can travel through several paths

insights.claim: EMC is not a single component, shield, filter, or PCB-layout rule but a system property involving the IC, package, PCB, power network, connectors, cables, enclosure, firmware, and operating modes all contributing to the final result.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Implies that point fixes (a shield, a filter) are insufficient — investors evaluating hardware suppliers' reliability claims should look for evidence of system-level EMC design discipline, not isolated compliance certificates.
insights.quote: That is because EMC is not a single component, shield, filter, or PCB-layout rule. It is a system property.
insights.locator: But none of those actions alone

insights.claim: Fixing one local EMI problem can unintentionally move the energy elsewhere: a shield can redirect current into a cable, a filter can create a resonance, a grounding change can alter the return path, and a package improvement can expose a board-level weakness.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Warns that piecemeal EMI fixes can create new failure modes elsewhere in the system — a risk factor for hardware product recalls or field failures if compliance is treated superficially.
insights.quote: A design can solve one local EMI problem and unintentionally move the energy elsewhere. A shield can redirect current into a cable. A filter can create a resonance.
insights.locator: A design can solve one local EMI problem

insights.claim: A product may pass EMC testing in one operating mode and fail when software changes the switching pattern, load transient, clock state, or interface activity, which is why end-of-line compliance testing alone is insufficient.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Firmware/software updates can silently break EMC compliance post-certification — a hidden operational risk for any connected/electronic product company relying on OTA updates.
insights.quote: A product may pass in one operating mode and fail when software changes the switching pattern, load transient, clock state, or interface activity. This is why compliance testing at the end is not enough.
insights.locator: A product may pass in one operating mode

insights.claim: The recommended approach is to engineer EMC from the beginning: understand sources, map coupling paths, define sensitive victims, model package/board behavior, control return currents, co-design grounding/filtering/shielding/placement, and test realistic operating states before collecting evidence of reliable system performance.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A prescriptive design methodology that hardware/chip companies can be benchmarked against; firms with this discipline built in early likely have lower field-failure and recall risk than those retrofitting compliance.
insights.quote: The better approach is to engineer EMC from the beginning: Understand likely sources. Map coupling paths. Define sensitive victims. Model package and board behavior.
insights.locator: The better approach is to engineer EMC

insights.claim: Emissions and immunity are distinct questions — emissions asks what unwanted energy a product generates, immunity asks whether the product keeps working when exposed to external disturbance — and true compatibility requires satisfying both.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Clarifies two separate compliance dimensions that must both be tested, useful for evaluating the rigor of a supplier's EMC test regime.
insights.quote: Emissions ask: What unwanted energy does the product generate? Immunity asks: Can the product keep working when exposed to an external disturbance? Compatibility requires both.
insights.locator: There is also another common confusion
